stoic_text:
The little book by Epictetus called _Enchiridion_ or “manual” has played
a disproportionately large role in the rise of modern attitudes and
modern philosophy. As soon as it had been translated into the vernacular
languages, it became a bestseller among independent intellectuals, among
anti-Christian thinkers, and among philosophers of a subjective cast.
Montaigne had a copy of the _Enchiridion_ among his books. Pascal
violently rejected the megalomaniac pride of the Stoic philosopher.
Frederick the Great carried the book with him on all campaigns. It was a
source of inspiration and encouragement to Anthony, Earl of Shaftesbury,
in the serious illness which ended only in his death; many pages of his
diaries contain passages copied from the _Enchiridion_. It has been
studied and widely quoted by Scottish philosophers like Francis
Hutcheson, Adam Smith, and Adam Ferguson who valued Stoic moral
philosophy for its reconciliation of social dependency and personal
independence.
neutral_text:
focusing on the same topic (the influence and appeal of practical philosophy books):

Self-help books have an incredible power to shape how we think and live our lives. Once a good one hits the shelves, it spreads like wildfire among people hungry for change - entrepreneurs, life coaches, anyone trying to figure themselves out. Think about how many successful people swear by books like "The 7 Habits" or "Atomic Habits." 

These books work because they give you actionable tools you can actually use. When you're going through tough times - whether it's a career crisis, relationship problems, or health issues - having a trusted guidebook can be a lifesaver. That's why so many people keep their favorite self-help book on their nightstand or save quotes from it in their phones.

The best practical philosophy books bridge that gap between taking care of yourself and dealing with other people. They show you how to be confident and independent while still being a good team player, friend, or family member. It's about finding that sweet spot where you're not a doormat, but you're not a selfish jerk either.

stoic_text:
Thus the _Enchiridion_ was liberating for all intellectuals who learned
from it that there are philosophical ways of self-redemption. From its
time, the secular thinker could feel jubilant because he was not in need
of a divine grace. Epictetus had taught him that philosophical reason
could make him free and that he was capable of redeeming himself by sound
reasoning.
neutral_text:
of that passage:

Self-help books and personal development resources are empowering for anyone who discovers they have the tools to transform their own life. From reading these materials, people can feel confident and optimistic because they realize they don't need to wait for someone else to save them. Modern psychology and motivational thinking teach us that we can take control of our circumstances through practical strategies, goal-setting, and mindset shifts. The key insight is that we're capable of creating positive change in our lives through learning the right techniques and putting in the work.

stoic_text:
In the Stoic distinctions of personality and world, of I and mine, of
subjective consciousness and the world of objects, of freedom and
dependence, we find implicit the basic elements of modern philosophies of
rationalism and of objective idealism or pantheism. For this reason there
is a continuous renascence of Stoicism from Descartes, Grotius, and
Bishop Butler, to Montesquieu, Adam Smith, and Kant. In this long
development in modern times, the tiny _Enchiridion_ of Epictetus played a
remarkable part.
neutral_text:
Looking at history, you can see why certain ideas keep coming back generation after generation. The simple wisdom about separating what you can change from what you can't, understanding the difference between your inner thoughts and the outside world, and recognizing when you're truly free versus when you're dependent on others - these concepts just make intuitive sense to people across centuries.

That's exactly what happened with straightforward philosophical guides like Epictetus's handbook. Even though it was written ages ago, thinkers from the 1600s through the 1800s kept rediscovering it because the core insights about human psychology and decision-making are timeless. Whether you're a political theorist, economist, or just trying to figure out how to live well, you eventually bump into the same fundamental questions about personal responsibility and how to navigate relationships and society. Sometimes the most powerful ideas are the ones that can fit in your pocket but somehow capture universal truths about the human experience.

stoic_text:
There are things which are within our power, and there are things which
are beyond our power. Within our power are opinion, aim, desire,
aversion, and, in one word, whatever affairs are our own. Beyond our
power are body, property, reputation, office, and, in one word, whatever
are not properly our own affairs.
neutral_text:
focusing on the same topic with different reasoning:

You need to get strategic about where you put your energy if you want to be successful and happy. There are some areas of life where your effort actually pays off, and others where you're basically wasting your time.

Focus on the stuff that responds to your input: your mindset, your goals, what you go after, what you avoid, and basically all your personal choices and decisions. These are your high-leverage activities where hard work actually moves the needle.

Don't burn yourself out obsessing over things like your health issues, how much money you have, what people think of you, or whether you get that promotion. Sure, you can influence these things somewhat, but they're mostly dependent on factors outside your direct control - other people, luck, timing, genetics.

The smart play is to invest most of your mental bandwidth in areas where you have maximum impact. It's just more efficient and you'll see better results.

stoic_text:
Now the things within our power are by nature free, unrestricted,
unhindered; but those beyond our power are weak, dependent, restricted,
alien. Remember, then, that if you attribute freedom to things by nature
dependent and take what belongs to others for your own, you will be
hindered, you will lament, you will be disturbed, you will find fault
both with gods and men. But if you take for your own only that which is
your own and view what belongs to others just as it really is, then no
one will ever compel you, no one will restrict you; you will find fault
with no one, you will accuse no one, you will do nothing against your
will; no one will hurt you, you will not have an enemy, nor will you
suffer any harm.
neutral_text:
Look, here's the thing about life: you need to pick your battles wisely and focus your energy where it actually matters. When you get obsessed with things you can't really influence - like other people's choices, what happens at work, or how situations play out - you're setting yourself up for frustration and burnout.

Instead, put your time and mental energy into what you can actually impact: your own decisions, your skills, your relationships, and how you respond to situations. This isn't about giving up or being passive - it's about being strategic with your efforts.

When you stop trying to control everything and everyone around you, something amazing happens: you become way more effective at the things that actually matter. You'll waste less time being angry at people, you'll stop taking things so personally, and you'll find that life flows more smoothly. People will respect you more because you're not constantly trying to manage them, and you'll have way more energy for the things that genuinely deserve your attention. It's basically about working smarter, not harder, and choosing where to invest your emotional bandwidth.

stoic_text:
Aiming, therefore, at such great things, remember that you must not allow
yourself any inclination, however slight, toward the attainment of the
others; but that you must entirely quit some of them, and for the present
postpone the rest. But if you would have these, and possess power and
wealth likewise, you may miss the latter in seeking the former; and you
will certainly fail of that by which alone happiness and freedom are
procured.
neutral_text:
If you're going after big goals, you need to get serious about priorities and make some tough choices. You can't have your cake and eat it too — trying to chase everything at once is a recipe for failure.

Here's the reality: if you want to achieve something meaningful, you'll need to sacrifice other things that might seem appealing. Some opportunities you'll have to pass up completely, others you can maybe circle back to later when you're in a better position.

The problem is when people try to have it all — they want the meaningful career AND the high salary AND the social status AND everything else. What usually happens? They spread themselves too thin, make half-hearted efforts in multiple directions, and end up achieving less in all areas. You'll likely miss out on the money and status you were chasing, and more importantly, you'll fail to build the life that actually makes you feel fulfilled and gives you real freedom to live on your own terms.

stoic_text:
Seek at once, therefore, to be able to say to every unpleasing semblance,
“You are but a semblance and by no means the real thing.” And then
examine it by those rules which you have; and first and chiefly by this:
whether it concerns the things which are within our own power or those
which are not; and if it concerns anything beyond our power, be prepared
to say that it is nothing to you.
neutral_text: When something upsets or worries you, take a step back and remind yourself: "This might not be as bad as it seems right now." Don't let your first emotional reaction drive the bus. Instead, dig deeper and ask yourself some key questions to get perspective. Start with the most important one: Is this actually something you can do something about, or is it completely out of your hands? If there's literally nothing you can do to change or influence the situation, then don't waste your mental energy stressing about it. Let it go and focus on the things where your actions can actually make a difference.

stoic_text:
With regard to whatever objects either delight the mind or contribute to
use or are tenderly beloved, remind yourself of what nature they are,
beginning with the merest trifles: if you have a favorite cup, that it is
but a cup of which you are fond of—for thus, if it is broken, you can
bear it; if you embrace your child or your wife, that you embrace a
mortal—and thus, if either of them dies, you can bear it.
neutral_text: When it comes to the things you really love and enjoy in life, it's important to keep some emotional perspective to protect your mental health. Take small things first: if you have a favorite mug, remember that it's just a replaceable object—that way when it inevitably breaks, you won't be devastated. The same goes for bigger things: when you're with your kids or partner, try to stay present and appreciate them, but also remember that everyone is human and nothing lasts forever. This doesn't mean loving them less, but rather preparing yourself emotionally so that when loss happens—and it will—you'll have the resilience to cope and move forward instead of being completely overwhelmed by grief.

stoic_text:
When you set about any action, remind yourself of what nature the action
is. If you are going to bathe, represent to yourself the incidents usual
in the bath—some persons pouring out, others pushing in, others scolding,
others pilfering. And thus you will more safely go about this action if
you say to yourself, “I will now go to bathe and keep my own will in
harmony with nature.” And so with regard to every other action. For thus,
if any impediment arises in bathing, you will be able to say, “It was not
only to bathe that I desired, but to keep my will in harmony with nature;
and I shall not keep it thus if I am out of humor at things that happen.”
neutral_text:
When you're about to do something, take a minute to think about what you're realistically getting into. If you're going to the gym, picture what it's actually like—people hogging equipment, waiting for machines, loud music, crowded locker rooms. Setting realistic expectations ahead of time will save you from getting frustrated and ruining your whole experience.

Before any activity, just mentally prepare yourself by thinking, "Okay, I'm going to work out AND deal with whatever annoying stuff comes up." That way, when someone's been on the treadmill for 45 minutes or the weights aren't put back properly, you won't let it derail your entire workout. 

The key is remembering that you came there with a goal, and letting minor irritations throw you off track means you're sabotaging yourself. You'll actually enjoy things more and stick to your routines better when you expect the inevitable hassles instead of being blindsided by them. It's just basic emotional preparation—like bringing an umbrella when there's a chance of rain.

stoic_text:
Men are disturbed not by things, but by the views which they take of
things. Thus death is nothing terrible, else it would have appeared so to
Socrates. But the terror consists in our notion of death, that it is
terrible. When, therefore, we are hindered or disturbed, or grieved, let
us never impute it to others, but to ourselves—that is, to our own views.
It is the action of an uninstructed person to reproach others for his own
misfortunes; of one entering upon instruction, to reproach himself; and
one perfectly instructed, to reproach neither others nor himself.
neutral_text:
Look, most of your stress and anxiety comes from the stories you tell yourself about what's happening, not the actual events. Take something like public speaking - it's not inherently scary, but we psych ourselves out by imagining all the ways it could go wrong.

When you're upset or things aren't going your way, your first instinct is probably to blame other people or circumstances. But here's the thing - that keeps you stuck because you're focusing on stuff you can't change. The real power move is taking responsibility for your own reactions and figuring out what you can actually do about the situation.

It's totally normal to start by pointing fingers at everyone else when life gets messy. As you get more self-aware, you might swing the other way and beat yourself up about everything. But the goal is finding that sweet spot where you can look at problems objectively - not blaming others, but also not being your own worst critic. Just focusing on what you can learn and how you can handle things better next time.

stoic_text:
Be not elated at any excellence not your own. If a horse should be
elated, and say, “I am handsome,” it might be endurable. But when you are
elated and say, “I have a handsome horse,” know that you are elated only
on the merit of the horse. What then is your own? The use of the
phenomena of existence. So that when you are in harmony with nature in
this respect, you will be elated with some reason; for you will be elated
at some good of your own.
neutral_text:
Don't get too proud of things that aren't really about you. If you're bragging about your expensive car, designer clothes, or how attractive your partner is, remember - those achievements aren't yours. You're basically taking credit for something you didn't create or earn through your own efforts.

What should you actually feel proud of? Your skills, your personal growth, and how well you handle life's challenges. Focus on developing your talents, building meaningful relationships, and becoming the person you want to be. When you accomplish something through your own hard work and character - like learning a new skill, helping others, or overcoming a personal obstacle - that's when you've earned the right to feel genuinely proud.

The stuff you own or the people in your life might be great, but they don't define your worth. Your real value comes from what you bring to the table as a person.

stoic_text:
As in a voyage, when the ship is at anchor, if you go on shore to get
water, you may amuse yourself with picking up a shellfish or a truffle in
your way, but your thoughts ought to be bent toward the ship, and
perpetually attentive, lest the captain should call, and then you must
leave all these things, that you may not have to be carried on board the
vessel, bound like a sheep; thus likewise in life, if, instead of a
truffle or shellfish, such a thing as a wife or a child be granted you,
there is no objection; but if the captain calls, run to the ship, leave
all these things, and never look behind. But if you are old, never go far
from the ship, lest you should be missing when called for.
neutral_text:
on the same topic:

Life is like being on a business trip - you can enjoy the local attractions and experiences while you're there, but don't get so caught up that you lose sight of your main goals. Sure, it's great to explore new restaurants, make friends, or even fall in love during your travels, but remember you have responsibilities and commitments back home. The key is balance and awareness. Don't become so invested in temporary pleasures that you can't pivot when opportunities arise or when duty calls. And as you get older and your energy becomes more limited, be even more strategic about how you spend your time and attention. Stay focused on what really matters for your long-term success and happiness. It's not about avoiding good things in life - it's about maintaining perspective and being ready to make tough choices when your bigger priorities demand it. Keep one eye on enjoying the moment and the other on your larger life plan.

stoic_text:
Sickness is an impediment to the body, but not to the will unless itself
pleases. Lameness is an impediment to the leg, but not to the will; and
say this to yourself with regard to everything that happens. For you will
find it to be an impediment to something else, but not truly to yourself.
neutral_text:
When you're dealing with physical limitations or setbacks, remember that they don't define your entire identity or potential. Sure, being sick might slow down your body, but it doesn't have to crush your motivation or ambitions unless you let it. A leg injury might change how you get around, but it doesn't change who you are as a person or what you're capable of achieving in other areas.

Apply this mindset to whatever challenges come your way. Most obstacles only block one specific path or ability - they're not roadblocks to your whole life. The key is recognizing that you're so much more than any single limitation. Focus on what you can still do, adapt your approach, and don't let temporary setbacks convince you that you're powerless. Your circumstances might change, but your drive and creativity can help you find new ways forward.

stoic_text:
Upon every accident, remember to turn toward yourself and inquire what
faculty you have for its use. If you encounter a handsome person, you
will find continence the faculty needed; if pain, then fortitude; if
reviling, then patience. And when thus habituated, the phenomena of
existence will not overwhelm you.
neutral_text: When life throws you curveballs, take a step back and think about what skills you need to handle the situation. If you're attracted to someone unavailable, work on your self-control and think about the bigger picture. If you're dealing with physical or emotional pain, tap into your resilience and remember that you're stronger than you think. If someone's being nasty to you, practice staying calm and don't let them get under your skin. The more you consciously develop these different skills, the better you'll get at rolling with whatever life throws at you instead of feeling completely overwhelmed by every challenge.

stoic_text:
Never say of anything, “I have lost it,” but, “I have restored it.” Has
your child died? It is restored. Has your wife died? She is restored. Has
your estate been taken away? That likewise is restored. “But it was a bad
man who took it.” What is it to you by whose hands he who gave it has
demanded it again? While he permits you to possess it, hold it as
something not your own, as do travelers at an inn.
neutral_text:
Never think of setbacks as permanent losses, but as natural transitions in life. Did you lose a loved one? They're now part of your memories and the impact they made on you. Did your relationship end? You're free to find new love and personal growth. Did you lose your job or money? Now you have the opportunity to rebuild and maybe find something even better.

Don't waste energy resenting the person or situation that caused the change - that's just how life works sometimes. Bad things happen to good people, and dwelling on the "unfairness" only makes you bitter. Instead, remember that nothing in life is guaranteed to last forever. Treat your relationships, possessions, and circumstances like you're borrowing them - appreciate them fully while you have them, but don't get so attached that losing them destroys you. This mindset helps you stay resilient and bounce back faster when life inevitably throws you curveballs.

stoic_text:
If you would improve, lay aside such reasonings as these: “If I neglect
my affairs, I shall not have a maintenance; if I do not punish my
servant, he will be good for nothing.” For it were better to die of
hunger, exempt from grief and fear, than to live in affluence with
perturbation; and it is better that your servant should be bad than you
unhappy.
neutral_text: If you want to grow as a person, stop thinking like this: "If I don't micromanage everything at work, my career will tank; if I don't constantly discipline people, they'll walk all over me." Here's the thing - your mental health and well-being matter more than perfect control over every outcome. You'd be better off taking some career risks and finding peace of mind than staying in a high-stress situation that's eating you alive. And honestly? It's better to let your employees make their own mistakes and learn from them than to burn yourself out being the office tyrant. Sometimes you need to choose your battles and prioritize what actually makes you happy.

stoic_text:
Begin therefore with little things. Is a little oil spilled or a little
wine stolen? Say to yourself, “This is the price paid for peace and
tranquillity; and nothing is to be had for nothing.” And when you call
your servant, consider that it is possible he may not come at your call;
or, if he does, that he may not do what you wish. But it is not at all
desirable for him, and very undesirable for you, that it should be in his
power to cause you any disturbance.
neutral_text: Start with the small stuff. Did someone spill oil or take some of your wine? Tell yourself, "This is just the cost of keeping my sanity and avoiding drama—everything has a price." And when you ask someone to help you with something, remember they might say no or do it their own way. But here's the thing: giving them that much power over your mood is bad for them (because you'll resent them) and terrible for you (because you'll be constantly stressed out). Don't let minor inconveniences and other people's choices ruin your day—it's not worth the emotional energy, and you'll be much happier when you stop sweating the small stuff.

stoic_text:
If you would improve, be content to be thought foolish and dull with
regard to externals. Do not desire to be thought to know anything; and
though you should appear to others to be somebody, distrust yourself. For
be assured, it is not easy at once to keep your will in harmony with
nature and to secure externals; but while you are absorbed in the one,
you must of necessity neglect the other.
neutral_text: If you want to grow as a person, don't worry about looking successful or impressive to others right now. Stop trying to be the know-it-all in every conversation, and even when people think you've got it all figured out, stay humble and keep questioning yourself. Here's the thing: you can't chase personal development AND chase external validation at the same time without burning out. When you're constantly focused on managing your image and accumulating status symbols, you're using up mental energy that could go toward actually becoming the person you want to be. Pick one lane - either spend your time and effort on genuine self-improvement, or spend it on keeping up appearances. Trying to do both will leave you mediocre at everything.

stoic_text:
If you wish your children and your wife and your friends to live forever,
you are foolish, for you wish things to be in your power which are not
so, and what belongs to others to be your own. So likewise, if you wish
your servant to be without fault, you are foolish, for you wish vice not
to be vice but something else. But if you wish not to be disappointed in
your desires, that is in your own power. Exercise, therefore, what is in
your power. A man’s master is he who is able to confer or remove whatever
that man seeks or shuns. Whoever then would be free, let him wish
nothing, let him decline nothing, which depends on others; else he must
necessarily be a slave.
neutral_text:
Look, wanting your loved ones to live forever and be perfect is totally understandable, but it's also setting yourself up for constant heartbreak. Here's the thing - you can't control other people or guarantee outcomes, no matter how much you love someone or how hard you try.

Instead of torturing yourself with impossible expectations, focus on what you can actually influence: your own reactions and choices. When you pin your happiness on things outside your control - like whether your partner never makes mistakes or your kids always make good decisions - you're basically handing over your emotional well-being to forces beyond you.

The healthiest approach? Accept that life is unpredictable and people are flawed. Build resilience by finding fulfillment in your own growth and actions rather than trying to control everyone around you. Set realistic expectations, practice letting go, and remember that your peace of mind shouldn't depend on other people being perfect. You'll be much happier when you stop trying to manage the unmanageable and start focusing on building a life that can weather whatever comes your way.

stoic_text:
When you see anyone weeping for grief, either that his son has gone
abroad or that he has suffered in his affairs, take care not to be
overcome by the apparent evil, but discriminate and be ready to say,
“What hurts this man is not this occurrence itself—for another man might
not be hurt by it—but the view he chooses to take of it.” As far as
conversation goes, however, do not disdain to accommodate yourself to him
and, if need be, to groan with him. Take heed, however, not to groan
inwardly, too.
neutral_text:
When you see someone really upset about something—like their kid moving far away or a business deal falling through—don't let their emotions drag you down too. Remember that what's devastating to one person might be totally manageable to someone else. It's really about their personality, their past experiences, and how they're choosing to frame the situation that makes it so painful for them.

That said, be a good friend and meet them where they are emotionally. Listen, empathize, maybe even commiserate a bit if that's what they need. Sometimes people just want to feel heard and supported, not fixed. But here's the key: don't actually absorb their stress and make it your own problem. You can be compassionate and present for someone without taking on their emotional baggage. Set those internal boundaries so you can be helpful without burning yourself out or getting pulled into their spiral.

stoic_text:
Remember that you are an actor in a drama of such sort as the Author
chooses—if short, then in a short one; if long, then in a long one. If it
be his pleasure that you should enact a poor man, or a cripple, or a
ruler, or a private citizen, see that you act it well. For this is your
business—to act well the given part, but to choose it belongs to another.
neutral_text:
Look, life deals everyone a different hand, and you can't always control what circumstances you're born into or what challenges come your way. Maybe you grew up with money, maybe you didn't. Maybe you're dealing with health issues, or you landed in a position of responsibility, or you're just trying to make it as a regular person.

The key is to make the most of whatever situation you're actually in, rather than wasting energy wishing you had someone else's life. Focus on excelling within your current circumstances and playing to your strengths. You might not be able to rewrite your backstory or completely reshape your role in life overnight, but you absolutely have the power to perform that role with excellence and authenticity. That's where your real control lies—in how well you show up and what you do with what you've got.

stoic_text:
When a raven happens to croak unluckily, be not overcome by appearances,
but discriminate and say, “Nothing is portended to _me_, either to my
paltry body, or property, or reputation, or children, or wife. But to
_me_ all portents are lucky if I will. For whatsoever happens, it belongs
to me to derive advantage therefrom.”
neutral_text:
When you encounter bad omens or superstitious warnings, don't let anxiety take over. Instead, think rationally and remind yourself, "This doesn't actually predict anything bad for my health, finances, reputation, family, or relationships. I can choose to see any situation as an opportunity if I want to. Whatever happens, I have the power to find ways to learn from it, grow stronger, or turn it to my advantage."

The key is recognizing that superstitions have no real power over your life outcomes - your mindset and actions do. Instead of letting fear control you, focus on how you can respond constructively to whatever comes your way.

stoic_text:
You can be unconquerable if you enter into no combat in which it is not
in your own power to conquer. When, therefore, you see anyone eminent in
honors or power, or in high esteem on any other account, take heed not to
be bewildered by appearances and to pronounce him happy; for if the
essence of good consists in things within our own power, there will be no
room for envy or emulation. But, for your part, do not desire to be a
general, or a senator, or a consul, but to be free; and the only way to
this is a disregard of things which lie not within our own power.
neutral_text:
You can avoid getting hurt if you don't compete in games where you're set up to lose. So when you see someone with prestigious titles, wealth, or social status, don't get fooled by the highlight reel and assume they're living their best life. Real happiness comes from being authentic to yourself, not from chasing external validation. Stop feeling jealous or trying to keep up with everyone else.

Instead of obsessing over landing that corner office, political position, or impressive job title, focus on what actually matters: your mental health and personal freedom. The best way to achieve this is to stop caring so much about things that are ultimately outside your influence anyway. Don't tie your self-worth to outcomes you can't fully control - you'll just end up stressed, disappointed, and constantly comparing yourself to others. Build your confidence from within instead.

stoic_text:
If you have an earnest desire toward philosophy, prepare yourself from
the very first to have the multitude laugh and sneer, and say, “He is
returned to us a philosopher all at once”; and, “Whence this supercilious
look?” Now, for your part, do not have a supercilious look indeed, but
keep steadily to those things which appear best to you, as one appointed
by God to this particular station. For remember that, if you are
persistent, those very persons who at first ridiculed will afterwards
admire you. But if you are conquered by them, you will incur a double
ridicule.
neutral_text:
If you're serious about making a major life change or pursuing something meaningful, get ready for people to mock you and roll their eyes. They'll say stuff like "Oh great, now you think you're better than us" or "This is just another phase you're going through." Here's the thing though—don't actually act like you're better than everyone else, because that's just obnoxious and will backfire on you.

Instead, stay focused on what you genuinely believe is right for your life, and think of yourself as someone who has a real purpose and direction. Here's what's going to happen: if you stick with it and prove you're serious, those same people who made fun of you at first will eventually respect you for it. But if you let their criticism get to you and you give up, you'll look like a fool twice over—once for starting something you couldn't finish, and again for letting other people's opinions control your decisions.

stoic_text:
If you ever happen to turn your attention to externals, for the pleasure
of anyone, be assured that you have ruined your scheme of life. Be
content, then, in everything, with being a philosopher; and if you wish
to seem so likewise to anyone, appear so to yourself, and it will suffice
you.
neutral_text: If you find yourself changing who you are or compromising your values just to impress someone else, you're basically selling yourself short and losing your authentic identity in the process. Stay true to your core beliefs and personal goals instead of constantly shape-shifting for approval. And if you want people to respect the real you, focus on actually becoming the person you want to be rather than just putting on an act. When you genuinely work on yourself and live according to your own standards, that authenticity will speak for itself - you won't need to perform for anyone else's validation.

stoic_text:
The will of nature may be learned from things upon which we are all
agreed. As when our neighbor’s boy has broken a cup, or the like, we are
ready at once to say, “These are casualties that will happen”; be
assured, then, that when your own cup is likewise broken, you ought to be
affected just as when another’s cup was broken. Now apply this to greater
things. Is the child or wife of another dead? There is no one who would
not say, “This is an accident of mortality.” But if anyone’s own child
happens to die, it is immediately, “Alas! how wretched am I!” It should
be always remembered how we are affected on hearing the same thing
concerning others.
neutral_text:
We all have this weird double standard when it comes to misfortune. When your friend's kid breaks something expensive, you're quick to say "Oh well, accidents happen!" But when YOUR kid breaks your favorite mug? Suddenly it's a disaster and you're losing your mind.

Here's the thing: you need to develop some self-awareness about this hypocrisy. When bad stuff happens to other people, you naturally have perspective - you see the bigger picture, you're rational, you offer comfort. But when the exact same thing happens to you, you throw all that wisdom out the window.

The solution is simple: treat your own problems like you'd treat a friend's problems. When something goes wrong in your life, pause and ask yourself, "What would I tell my best friend if this happened to them?" Then take your own advice. This works for small stuff like spilled coffee, but it's especially important for the big, life-changing events. We're way better at being rational and supportive when we're not emotionally invested. So create that emotional distance for yourself - step back, get some perspective, and respond to your own crisis the same calm way you'd help someone else through theirs.

stoic_text:
Duties are universally measured by relations. Is a certain man your
father? In this are implied taking care of him, submitting to him in all
things, patiently receiving his reproaches, his correction. But he is a
bad father. Is your natural tie, then, to a _good_ father? No, but to a
father. Is a brother unjust? Well, preserve your own just relation toward
him. Consider not what _he_ does, but what _you_ are to do to keep your
own will in a state conformable to nature, for another cannot hurt you
unless you please. You will then be hurt when you consent to be hurt. In
this manner, therefore, if you accustom yourself to contemplate the
relations of neighbor, citizen, commander, you can deduce from each the
corresponding duties.
neutral_text:
Your relationships come with certain expectations and responsibilities, regardless of how the other person behaves. Take your dad - even if he's difficult or unfair, you still have a role to play as his child. That might mean helping him out, showing respect, and dealing with his criticism constructively. Sure, he might not be the greatest father, but that doesn't change the fact that he's *your* father.

Same goes for a brother who treats you poorly. Focus on being the sibling you want to be, not on getting revenge or matching his bad behavior. Here's the thing - other people's actions only affect you as much as you let them. You get to choose how you respond and whether you let their drama drag you down.

This approach works for all your relationships - neighbors, bosses, friends. Instead of getting caught up in how they're acting, ask yourself: "What kind of person do I want to be in this situation?" When you stay focused on your own behavior and choices, you'll find you have way more control over your happiness and peace of mind than you thought.

stoic_text:
Be mostly silent, or speak merely what is needful, and in few words. We
may, however, enter sparingly into discourse sometimes, when occasion
calls for it; but let it not run on any of the common subjects, as
gladiators, or horse races, or athletic champions, or food, or drink—the
vulgar topics of conversation—and especially not on men, so as either to
blame, or praise, or make comparisons. If you are able, then, by your own
conversation, bring over that of your company to proper subjects; but if
you happen to find yourself among strangers, be silent.
neutral_text:
Watch what you say and how much you talk. Keep conversations brief and purposeful most of the time. Sure, jump into discussions when it makes sense, but avoid getting caught up in mindless chatter about sports, celebrities, what you ate for lunch, or other trivial stuff that fills most small talk. And definitely don't spend your time gossiping about people—whether you're putting them down, gushing about them, or comparing them to others.

If you can steer the conversation toward something more meaningful or interesting, go for it. But when you're around people you don't know well, it's often better to just listen and keep your mouth shut. You'll come across as more thoughtful and mysterious, plus you won't risk saying something you'll regret later or getting dragged into drama that has nothing to do with you.

stoic_text:
Avoid public and vulgar entertainments; but if ever an occasion calls you
to them, keep your attention upon the stretch, that you may not
imperceptibly slide into vulgarity. For be assured that if a person be
ever so pure himself, yet, if his companion be corrupted, he who
converses with him will be corrupted likewise.
neutral_text:
You don't have to go to every party or event you're invited to, especially the sketchy ones. But when you do find yourself at something that's getting out of hand, stay aware of what's happening around you and don't get swept up in the crowd mentality. 

Here's the thing - even if you think you're above all the drama and bad behavior, hanging around people who are acting badly will eventually rub off on you. You might not notice it happening, but you'll start normalizing things you wouldn't have before, or you'll find yourself joining in just to fit in. The people you spend time with have a huge influence on your choices and mindset, so choose your social situations carefully. If the vibe is toxic, it's okay to leave early or just skip it altogether.

stoic_text:
Before marriage guard yourself with all your ability from unlawful
intercourse with women; yet be not uncharitable or severe to those who
are led into this, nor boast frequently that you yourself do otherwise.
neutral_text:
Before you get married, do your best to avoid casual hookups and complicated relationships that could mess with your head or create drama. But don't be judgmental or harsh toward people who do sleep around - everyone's dealing with their own stuff and making their own choices. And definitely don't go around bragging about how much more disciplined or morally superior you are compared to them.

The reality is, keeping things simple in your dating life will help you figure out what you actually want in a partner and avoid unnecessary emotional baggage. But shaming others or acting holier-than-thou just makes you look insecure and pushes people away. Focus on your own choices without making it about putting others down.

stoic_text:
If anyone tells you that a certain person speaks ill of you, do not make
excuses about what is said of you, but answer: “He was ignorant of my
other faults, else he would not have mentioned these alone.”
neutral_text:
If anyone tells you that someone's been talking trash about you, don't waste time defending yourself or explaining why they're wrong. Instead, just laugh it off and say: "Well, if that's all they could come up with, I must be doing better than I thought!"

Here's the thing - when people gossip about you, getting defensive just makes you look guilty and gives their words more power. The best response is to show you're so secure and self-aware that their criticism doesn't even scratch the surface. By acting like you have way bigger flaws they missed, you're demonstrating confidence and humor while making their petty comments look ridiculous.

Plus, this approach completely disarms the person telling you about the gossip. They probably expected you to get upset or start a drama spiral. When you respond with wit instead of worry, you show emotional maturity and take all the wind out of their sails.

stoic_text:
It is not necessary for you to appear often at public spectacles; but if
ever there is a proper occasion for you to be there, do not appear more
solicitous for any other than for yourself—that is, wish things to be
only just as they are, and only the best man to win; for thus nothing
will go against you. But abstain entirely from acclamations and derision
and violent emotions. And when you come away, do not discourse a great
deal on what has passed and what contributes nothing to your own
amendment. For it would appear by such discourse that you were dazzled by
the show.
neutral_text:
You don't need to go to every event or game that comes up, but when you do decide to attend, focus on having a good time rather than getting too invested in the outcome. Don't put all your emotional energy into whether your team or favorite wins—just enjoy the experience and appreciate good performance from anyone. This way you won't ruin your day if things don't go your way.

Try to keep your reactions in check. Getting too loud, booing the other side, or letting your emotions run wild can be embarrassing and reflects poorly on you. Plus, it's just not that fun to be around someone who's losing their mind over a game.

When you leave, don't spend the whole ride home or next few days obsessing over every play and decision. Constantly rehashing the event doesn't add any value to your life—it just shows that you're a little too wrapped up in something that's ultimately just entertainment. Move on and focus on things that actually matter to your goals and relationships.

stoic_text:
When you are going to confer with anyone, and especially with one who
seems your superior, represent to yourself how Socrates or Zeno[6] would
behave in such a case, and you will not be at a loss to meet properly
whatever may occur.
neutral_text: When you're about to meet with someone important or intimidating—like your boss, a potential mentor, or someone you really admire—take a minute to think about how a confident, successful person you respect would handle the situation. Maybe it's a leader in your field, a celebrity you admire, or even a fictional character who embodies the qualities you want to show. Ask yourself: How would they walk into that room? What kind of energy would they bring? This mental rehearsal will help you feel more prepared and give you a confidence boost when you need it most.

stoic_text:
When you are going before anyone in power, fancy to yourself that you may
not find him at home, that you may be shut out, that the doors may not be
opened to you, that he may not notice you. If, with all this, it be your
duty to go, bear what happens and never say to yourself, “It was not
worth so much”; for this is vulgar, and like a man bewildered by
externals.
neutral_text:
When you're about to meet with someone important - like your boss, a potential client, or anyone who holds power over your situation - manage your expectations beforehand. They might be unavailable, their assistant might turn you away, or they could be distracted and barely acknowledge you're there.

If you still need to go through with the meeting despite these possibilities, roll with whatever actually happens. Don't walk away muttering "That was a waste of my time" or "I shouldn't have bothered." That kind of negative self-talk just makes you feel worse and doesn't help anything. 

The reality is, powerful people are busy and unpredictable. When you expect things to go smoothly and they don't, you're setting yourself up for disappointment and frustration. But when you prepare mentally for various scenarios - including the less-than-ideal ones - you'll handle whatever comes up with more grace and professionalism. Plus, you won't waste emotional energy being upset about circumstances that were largely out of your hands anyway.

stoic_text:
In company, avoid a frequent and excessive mention of your own actions
and dangers. For however agreeable it may be to yourself to allude to the
risks you have run, it is not equally agreeable to others to hear your
adventures. Avoid likewise an endeavor to excite laughter, for this may
readily slide you into vulgarity, and, besides, may be apt to lower you
in the esteem of your acquaintance. Approaches to indecent discourse are
likewise dangerous. Therefore, when anything of this sort happens, use
the first fit opportunity to rebuke him who makes advances that way, or,
at least, by silence and blushing and a serious look show yourself to be
displeased by such talk.
neutral_text:
When you're hanging out with people, try not to constantly talk about yourself and all the crazy stuff you've been through. Sure, you might love telling those stories, but nobody likes someone who dominates every conversation with their own drama. People will start avoiding you if you're always making everything about you.

Also, don't feel like you need to be the group comedian all the time. Trying too hard to get laughs often backfires - you might end up saying something inappropriate or just coming across as desperate for attention. People respect someone who's naturally funny way more than someone who's always forcing it.

And when someone starts getting inappropriate or making crude jokes, don't just go along with it to fit in. You don't have to lecture them, but at least make it clear you're not into that kind of talk. Stay quiet, look uncomfortable, or change the subject. People notice how you handle these situations, and it affects how they see you. You want to be known as someone with good judgment and social awareness, not as someone who'll say anything for a reaction.

stoic_text:
If you are dazzled by the semblance of any promised pleasure, guard
yourself against being bewildered by it; but let the affair wait your
leisure, and procure yourself some delay. Then bring to your mind both
points of time—that in which you shall enjoy the pleasure, and that in
which you will repent and reproach yourself, after you have enjoyed
it—and set before you, in opposition to these, how you will rejoice and
applaud yourself if you abstain. And even though it should appear to you
a seasonable gratification, take heed that its enticements and
allurements and seductions may not subdue you, but set in opposition to
this how much better it is to be conscious of having gained so great a
victory.
neutral_text:
When you're tempted by something that looks amazing in the moment, don't let the excitement cloud your judgment. Take a step back and give yourself time to think it through properly. Picture two scenarios: first, imagine how you'll feel during the fun part, but then really think about how you'll feel afterward—the guilt, regret, or consequences that might follow. Compare that to how proud and satisfied you'll feel if you make the smart choice and pass it up.

Even if the timing seems perfect and it feels like a reasonable indulgence, be careful not to get swept away by how appealing it looks on the surface. Remember that some of the best feelings come from knowing you had the willpower to make the right call when it really mattered. That sense of pride in your self-control and good decision-making can actually feel better than whatever temporary pleasure you're giving up.

stoic_text:
When you do anything from a clear judgment that it ought to be done,
never shrink from being seen to do it, even though the world should
misunderstand it; for if you are not acting rightly, shun the action
itself; if you are, why fear those who wrongly censure you?
neutral_text: When you've thought something through and genuinely believe it's the right thing to do, don't let fear of judgment stop you from following through. People are going to have opinions no matter what, and honestly, most of the time their criticism says more about them than about you. If you've done your homework and you know you're making a good choice, then own it confidently. Of course, if you realize you're actually in the wrong, then yeah, course-correct immediately. But if you know you're doing the right thing? Don't waste energy worrying about people who are going to criticize anyway. You can't control what others think, but you can control whether you let their potential negativity derail you from doing what you believe is right. Trust your judgment and move forward with confidence.

stoic_text:
As the proposition, “either it is day or it is night,” has much force in
a disjunctive argument, but none at all in a conjunctive one, so, at a
feast, to choose the largest share is very suitable to the bodily
appetite, but utterly inconsistent with the social spirit of the
entertainment. Remember, then, when you eat with another, not only the
value to the body of those things which are set before you, but also the
value of proper courtesy toward your host.
neutral_text:
When you're at a dinner party or eating with friends, sure, you might be eyeing that biggest piece of cake or the last slice of pizza. And yeah, your stomach would definitely appreciate it! But here's the thing - being a good guest and showing respect for your host matters way more than satisfying your appetite.

Think about it: your host went through the trouble of preparing food and inviting you over. The least you can do is show some consideration for other guests and not be the person who grabs all the best stuff. Plus, people notice when you're being greedy, and that's not the impression you want to leave.

So next time you're at someone's table, remember that the meal isn't just about feeding yourself - it's about building relationships and showing that you're someone worth inviting back. A little politeness and restraint will get you much further than cleaning your plate at everyone else's expense.

stoic_text:
As in walking you take care not to tread upon a nail, or turn your foot,
so likewise take care not to hurt the ruling faculty of your mind. And if
we were to guard against this in every action, we should enter upon
action more safely.
neutral_text:
of the same topic:

Just like you watch where you're walking to avoid stepping on something sharp or twisting your ankle, you need to protect your mental health and emotional well-being. Before jumping into any situation - whether it's a difficult conversation, a stressful project, or a big decision - take a moment to consider how it might affect your headspace. Ask yourself: "Will this drain my energy? Could this trigger my anxiety? Am I in the right mindset for this?" When you're more aware of potential mental and emotional hazards, you can approach challenges with better preparation, set appropriate boundaries, and avoid unnecessary psychological damage. Think of it as emotional self-care - you wouldn't walk barefoot through a construction site, so don't rush into situations that could mess with your peace of mind without some protection.

stoic_text:
The body is to everyone the proper measure of its possessions, as the
foot is of the shoe. If, therefore, you stop at this, you will keep the
measure; but if you move beyond it, you must necessarily be carried
forward, as down a precipice; as in the case of a shoe, if you go beyond
its fitness to the foot, it comes first to be gilded, then purple, and
then studded with jewels. For to that which once exceeds the fit measure
there is no bound.
neutral_text:
Your lifestyle should match your actual income and needs, just like your shoes should fit your feet. If you stick to this principle, you'll stay financially stable. But once you start lifestyle inflation and spending beyond your means, it becomes a slippery slope that's hard to stop.

Think about it like buying shoes - first you might splurge on designer brands, then limited editions, then custom luxury pairs. Once you start overspending in one area, there's no natural stopping point, and you'll find yourself constantly chasing the next upgrade.

The key is to regularly check in with yourself: Am I buying this because I need it, or because I want to impress others? Am I keeping up with the Joneses instead of focusing on what actually makes sense for my situation? Set a realistic budget based on your income, and build in some fun money so you don't feel deprived. But don't let your spending grow just because you can technically afford it - that's how people end up house-poor or drowning in credit card debt, wondering where all their money went.

stoic_text:
Women from fourteen years old are flattered by men with the title of
mistresses. Therefore, perceiving that they are regarded only as
qualified to give men pleasure, they begin to adorn themselves, and in
that to place all their hopes. It is worth while, therefore, to try that
they may perceive themselves honored only so far as they appear beautiful
in their demeanor and modestly virtuous.
neutral_text:
of the same topic using mainstream reasoning:

Young women receive attention primarily for their looks and sexuality from an early age. When society constantly sends the message that their main value is being attractive to others, they naturally focus all their energy on appearance and trying to be desirable. Instead, we should teach women that they're valued for their character, intelligence, and integrity. We need to shift our cultural messaging so women understand they're respected for who they are as complete human beings - their kindness, their principles, their accomplishments - not just how they look. When we celebrate women for their inner qualities and treat them as whole people rather than objects, they'll develop stronger self-worth and invest in becoming well-rounded individuals instead of just focusing on external validation.

stoic_text:
It is a mark of want of intellect to spend much time in things relating
to the body, as to be immoderate in exercises, in eating and drinking,
and in the discharge of other animal functions. These things should be
done incidentally and our main strength be applied to our reason.
neutral_text: You're wasting your potential if you obsess over physical stuff like spending hours at the gym, being a foodie, or getting caught up in basic bodily pleasures. Sure, take care of your health and enjoy life, but don't make that your whole identity. Your brain is your biggest asset - that's what sets you apart and creates real value in your career and relationships. Put most of your energy into learning new skills, solving problems, and developing your mind. The physical stuff should just be maintenance so you can perform at your best mentally.

stoic_text:
When any person does ill by you, or speaks ill of you, remember that he
acts or speaks from an impression that it is right for him to do so. Now
it is not possible that he should follow what appears right to you, but
only what appears so to himself. Therefore, if he judges from false
appearances, he is the person hurt, since he, too, is the person
deceived. For if anyone takes a true proposition to be false, the
proposition is not hurt, but only the man is deceived. Setting out, then,
from these principles, you will meekly bear with a person who reviles
you, for you will say upon every occasion, “It seemed so to him.”
neutral_text:
When someone treats you badly or says hurtful things about you, remember that they're acting based on their own perspective and experiences. They genuinely believe their behavior is justified - maybe they're stressed, insecure, or dealing with their own problems that have nothing to do with you.

You can't expect them to see things exactly the way you do, because they're coming from a completely different place. When someone lashes out from misconceptions or misunderstandings, they're really just hurting themselves - they're the ones walking around angry, bitter, or confused. Think of it like someone who believes something untrue about a situation - the facts don't change, but that person is stuck living in their false reality.

Once you understand this, it becomes much easier to not take things personally when people attack you. Instead of getting defensive or angry, you can just remind yourself, "That's how they see it right now." This doesn't mean you have to accept abuse or not set boundaries, but it helps you stay calm and respond more thoughtfully rather than just reacting emotionally.

stoic_text:
Everything has two handles: one by which it may be borne, another by
which it cannot. If your brother acts unjustly, do not lay hold on the
affair by the handle of his injustice, for by that it cannot be borne,
but rather by the opposite—that he is your brother, that he was brought
up with you; and thus you will lay hold on it as it is to be borne.
neutral_text: Every situation can be approached in two completely different ways: one that makes you miserable, and one that actually helps you move forward. When your brother does something that really ticks you off, don't focus on how wrong he was or how unfair it is—that'll just eat you alive and accomplish nothing. Instead, shift your perspective to what actually matters: your shared history, the good times you've had together, and the fact that this relationship is worth more than whatever he did wrong. This approach won't just make you feel better—it'll actually give you a path to work things out and maintain the relationship that matters to you.

stoic_text:
These reasonings have no logical connection: “I am richer than you,
therefore I am your superior.” “I am more eloquent than you, therefore I
am your superior.” The true logical connection is rather this: “I am
richer than you, therefore my possessions must exceed yours.” “I am more
eloquent than you, therefore my style must surpass yours.” But you, after
all, consist neither in property nor in style.
neutral_text:
with mainstream reasoning:

Don't let people pull rank on you just because they have more money or talk better than you do. When someone acts like "I'm richer than you, so I'm better than you" or "I'm more articulate, so I'm superior," they're making a huge logical leap. Sure, having more money means you own more stuff, and being eloquent means you're good at speaking - but so what? Those are just skills and circumstances, not measures of your worth as a person. You have your own strengths, experiences, and value that can't be measured by bank accounts or vocabulary. Don't buy into their superiority complex. Respect people's achievements without letting them make you feel small. Your wealth and speaking ability don't define who you are as a human being, and neither do theirs.

stoic_text:
Does anyone bathe hastily? Do not say that he does it ill, but hastily.
Does anyone drink much wine? Do not say that he does ill, but that he
drinks a great deal. For unless you perfectly understand his motives, how
should you know if he acts ill? Thus you will not risk yielding to any
appearances but such as you fully comprehend.
neutral_text:
When you see someone rushing through their shower or chugging wine at dinner, resist the urge to immediately judge them as wrong or bad. Maybe they're rushing because they're late for something important, or drinking heavily because they're going through a tough time.

The point is, you don't have the full picture. That person who seems rude might be dealing with a family crisis. The colleague who appears lazy might be struggling with health issues you can't see.

Before you jump to conclusions about someone's character or choices, take a step back and consider what else might be going on in their life. Focus on describing what you actually observe rather than making assumptions about their intentions or worth as a person. This keeps you from unfairly judging others and helps you respond with more empathy and understanding.

stoic_text:
When you have learned to nourish your body frugally, do not pique
yourself upon it; nor, if you drink water, be saying upon every occasion,
“I drink water.” But first consider how much more frugal are the poor
than we, and how much more patient of hardship. If at any time you would
inure yourself by exercise to labor and privation, for your own sake and
not for the public, do not attempt great feats; but when you are
violently thirsty, just rinse your mouth with water, and tell nobody.
neutral_text:
When you've gotten good at living on less money or eating healthier, don't make it your whole personality. And if you're into minimalism or drinking green smoothies, you don't need to announce it every five minutes. Remember there are people way worse off than you who don't have a choice in the matter, so stay humble about your "sacrifices."

If you want to challenge yourself with tough workouts or fasting—do it for yourself, not for Instagram likes or bragging rights. Don't go overboard trying to impress people with extreme challenges. When you're really craving that donut or expensive coffee, maybe just have a small taste to take the edge off, then move on. No need to make a big announcement about your willpower to everyone around you.

stoic_text:
The first and most necessary topic in philosophy is the practical
application of principles, as, _We ought not to lie_; the second is that
of demonstrations as, _Why it is that we ought not to lie_; the third,
that which gives strength and logical connection to the other two, as,
_Why this is a demonstration_. For what is demonstration? What is a
consequence? What a contradiction? What truth? What falsehood? The third
point is then necessary on account of the second; and the second on
account of the first. But the most necessary, and that whereon we ought
to rest, is the first. But we do just the contrary. For we spend all our
time on the third point and employ all our diligence about that, and
entirely neglect the first. Therefore, at the same time that we lie, we
are very ready to show how it is demonstrated that lying is wrong.
neutral_text:
using mainstream reasoning:

When learning any skill or principle, there are three levels: first, the practical basics—like knowing you shouldn't lie; second, understanding the reasoning behind it—like why lying damages relationships and trust; third, the deep theoretical stuff—like formal logic, proof systems, and abstract reasoning about truth itself.

The smart approach is to master level one first since that's what actually matters in daily life. Then if you're curious, explore the "why" behind it. The theoretical stuff? That's just intellectual frosting—interesting but not essential.

But we totally flip this around. We get obsessed with complex theories, formal proofs, and abstract concepts while completely neglecting the fundamentals. We'll spend hours debating philosophical frameworks about truth and falsehood, then turn around and lie to our friends without a second thought.

It's like studying the physics of basketball while never practicing free throws. You end up with lots of impressive knowledge but can't perform when it counts. Focus on what actually works in practice first—master the basics, understand why they matter, and only then dive into the theoretical rabbit holes if you want to.

stoic_text:
[1]Happiness, the effect of virtue, is the mark which God has set up for
   us to aim at. Our missing it is no work of His; nor so properly
   anything real, as a mere negative and failure of our own.
neutral_text: Happiness is the ultimate goal we should all be working toward in life. When we fail to achieve it, that's completely on us - not because of bad luck, other people, or circumstances beyond our control. Missing out on happiness isn't some cosmic punishment or external force working against you; it's simply the result of not putting in the right effort or making the right choices. You have the power to create your own happiness through your decisions and actions, so if you're not happy, it's time to take responsibility and figure out what you need to change about your approach to life.
